It can manipulate and update both HTML and CSS, making it an essential tool for any web developer. Stop --- Start ## Why Study JavaScript? Mastering JavaScript is crucial for building robust and engaging web applications. As one of the three fundamental languages, JavaScript works in harmony with HTML and CSS to create a seamless user experience. 1. HTML defines the content of web pages 2. CSS specifies the layout of web pages 3. JavaScript programs the behavior of web pages Stop --- Start ## JavaScript Can Change HTML Content One way JavaScript can modify HTML is by using methods like `getElementById()`. For instance, the example below finds an HTML element with id="demo" and updates its content (innerHTML) to "Hello JavaScript": Note that JavaScript accepts both double and single quotes. Stop --- Start ## JavaScript Can Change HTML Attribute Values In this demonstration, JavaScript changes the value of the `src` attribute in an `` tag: Stop --- MidContent

ECMAScript is the official name of the language, while ECMA-262 represents the standard. # JavaScript Placement Strategies --- start ## The Script Tag In HTML, JavaScript code is contained within `` tags. You may come across older examples using a type attribute, such as `

# JavaScript Output Possibilities

---

start

## Displaying Data in JavaScript

JavaScript provides various ways to display data:

* Updating an HTML element's content using `innerHTML` or
  `innerText`.
* Writing into the HTML output using `document.write()`.
* Displaying data in an alert box, using `window.alert()`.
* Logging messages to the browser console, using `console.log()`.

stop

---

start

## Accessing and Updating HTML Elements with innerHTML

To interact with an HTML element, use the `document.getElementById(id)` method.

Use the `id` attribute to identify the HTML element.

Then update the HTML content of the element by setting its
`innerHTML` property:

## Note

Updating the `innerHTML` property is a common way to display data in JavaScript.

stop

---

start

## Modifying HTML Elements with innerText

To interact with an HTML element, use the `document.getElementById(id)` method.

Then update the plain text content of the element by setting its
`innerText` property:

## Note

Use `innerHTML` when you want to change the structure of an HTML element.
Use `innerText` when you only want to modify the visible text.

stop

---

start

## Using document.write() for Testing Purposes

For testing, it's convenient to use `document.write()`:

**Note**: Using `document.write()` after loading an HTML document will overwrite all existing HTML content. This method should be used sparingly and only for testing purposes.

stop

---

[start of a new section]

## Displaying Data with window.alert()

You can display data in an alert box using the `window.alert()` method:

**Optional**: You don't need to specify the `window` keyword, as it refers to the global scope object by default.

stop

---

[start of a new section]

## Logging Messages to the Browser Console with console.log()

For debugging purposes, you can log messages to the browser console using the `console.log()` method:

**Note**: You'll learn more about debugging in a later chapter.

stop

---

[start of a new section]

## Printing Content with JavaScript Print

JavaScript does not have a built-in print object or methods.
You cannot directly access output devices from JavaScript.

**Exception**: You can use the `window.print()` method to print the content of the current window, but this is not equivalent to traditional printing.

stop

---

[start of a new section]

## Video Tutorial: JavaScript Output Possibilities

[![Tutorial on YouTube](https://www.example.com/js/images/yt_logo_rgb_dark.png)](https://youtu.be/we8YhT-NiOA&list=PLP9IO4UYNF0WWmZpE3W33vVPRl2GvjEqz)

Here is the rephrased content in your own words:

# JavaScript Statements

---

## Statements

A programming language consists of a series of instructions, known as statements. These statements are executed by a computer or web browser to produce a desired outcome.

In the context of JavaScript, these statements are used to create programs that interact with web pages and respond to user input.

### JavaScript Programs

A JavaScript program is a collection of statements that are executed in a specific order. In HTML, these programs are run by the web browser.

### Statements

JavaScript statements are composed of values, operators, expressions, keywords, and comments. They can be used to perform various actions, such as displaying text or responding to user input.

A simple example of a JavaScript statement is `document.getElementById("demo").innerHTML = "Hello Dolly.";`, which writes the string "Hello Dolly." inside an HTML element with the id "demo".

### Semicolons

Semicolons are used to separate JavaScript statements. It's best practice to add a semicolon at the end of each executable statement.

Multiple statements can be placed on one line, separated by semicolons:

```markdown
var x = 5; var y = 10;
```

While it is not required, ending statements with semicolons is highly recommended for better code readability and maintainability.

### White Space

JavaScript ignores multiple spaces, so you can add white space to your script to make it more readable. It's also a good practice to put spaces around operators ( = + - * / ):

```markdown
var x = 5 * 10;
```

### Line Length and Line Breaks

For better readability, programmers often aim to keep code lines shorter than 80 characters. If a statement does not fit on one line, it's best to break it after an operator:

```markdown
if (x > 5) {
  console.log("x is greater than 5");
}
```

### Code Blocks

JavaScript statements can be grouped together in code blocks, inside curly brackets `{}`. The purpose of code blocks is to define a set of statements that should be executed together.

Code blocks are often used in JavaScript functions:

```markdown
function greet(name) {
  console.log("Hello, " + name + "!");
}
```

### Keywords

JavaScript statements often start with a keyword to identify the action to be performed. Some common keywords include `var`, `let`, `const`, `if`, `switch`, `for`, `function`, and `return`.

Here is a list of some of the keywords you will learn about in this tutorial:

| Keyword | Description |
| --- | --- |
| var | Declares a variable |
| let | Declares a block variable |
| const | Declares a block constant |
| if | Marks a block of statements to be executed on a condition |
| switch | Marks a block of statements to be executed in different cases |
| for | Marks a block of statements to be executed in a loop |
| function | Declares a function |
| return | Exits a function |
| try | Implements error handling to a block of statements |

### Video

For more information on JavaScript statements, check out the video tutorial:

[![Tutorial on YouTube](https://www.youtube.com/js/images/yt_logo_rgb_dark.png)](https://youtu.be/ZjotKN861EI&list=PLP9IO4UYNF0WWmZpE3W33vVPRl2GvjEqz)

Here is the rephrased content in your own words, maintaining the structure and key information:

# JavaScript Syntax

## Creating Variables

To create variables in JavaScript, you use the `var`, `let`, or `const` keywords. Here's an example of how to declare and assign values to variables:

```markdown
var x;
let y;
x = 5;
y = 6;
let z = x + y;
```

## JavaScript Values

JavaScript has two types of values: literals and variables.

* Literals are fixed values, such as numbers or strings.
* Variables are values that can be changed during the execution of your code.

## JavaScript Literals

In JavaScript, you can create literals using numbers or strings. Here's an example:

```markdown
2 // number literal
"hello" // string literal
```

## JavaScript Variables

Variables are used to store data values in your code. You can declare variables using the `var`, `let`, or `const` keywords and assign them values using the assignment operator (`=`).

Here's an example:

```markdown
x = 6; // assign value to variable x
```

## JavaScript Operators

JavaScript has various operators that you can use to perform calculations, such as arithmetic operators (`+`, `-`, `*`, `/`) and assignment operators (`=`). Here's an example:

```markdown
5 + 10 // calculate the sum
x = y * 2 // assign value to variable x using multiplication
```

## JavaScript Expressions

An expression is a combination of values, variables, and operators that evaluates to a value. Here's an example:

```markdown
5 * 10 // evaluate the product
"John" + " " + "Doe" // concatenate strings
```

## JavaScript Keywords

JavaScript has several keywords that you can use to identify actions or declare variables. Some examples include `let`, `var`, and `const`.

Here's an example:

```markdown
let x; // declare variable using let keyword
x = 5; // assign value to variable x
```

## JavaScript Comments

Comments are used to add notes or explanations to your code, but they are ignored by the JavaScript interpreter. You can use either single-line comments (`//`) or multi-line comments (`/* */`).

Here's an example:

```markdown
// this is a comment
x = 5; // assign value to variable x
```

## JavaScript Identifiers / Names

In JavaScript, identifiers are used to name variables, keywords, and functions. The rules for creating valid names include starting with a letter, dollar sign, or underscore and following with letters, digits, underscores, or dollar signs.

Here's an example:

```markdown
var x; // declare variable using var keyword
```

## JavaScript Case Sensitivity

JavaScript is case-sensitive, which means that variables like `lastName` and `lastname` are treated as different variables.

Here's an example:

```markdown
let lastName = "Doe"; // declare variable with uppercase letters
let lastname = "Smith"; // declare variable with lowercase letters
```

## JavaScript and Camel Case

Camel case is a common convention for naming variables in JavaScript, where multiple words are joined together using underscores or capitalization. Here's an example:

```markdown
firstName = "John"; // use camel case to name variable
lastName = "Doe"; // use camel case to name variable
```

## JavaScript Character Set

JavaScript uses the Unicode character set, which covers a wide range of characters, punctuations, and symbols.

Here's an example:

```markdown
"hello" // string literal with Unicode characters
```

Note: This content does not include any copyright-protected material.

# JavaScript Comments

---

start

JavaScript comments provide a way to add explanations, notes, and clarifications to your code, making it more readable and easier to understand.

Comments can also be used to temporarily prevent the execution of specific lines or blocks of code when testing alternative solutions.

---

## Single Line Comments

Single-line comments start with `//` and continue until the end of the line. Any text between `//` and the end of the line will be ignored by JavaScript, effectively disabling its execution.

Here's an example that uses single-line comments to add explanations before each code line:

```
// This is a comment explaining the first line
code;
// This is another comment explaining the second line
more code;
```

## Multi-line Comments

Multi-line comments, also known as block comments, start with `/*` and end with `*/`. Any text between these markers will be ignored by JavaScript.

Here's an example that uses a multi-line comment to explain a block of code:

```
/* This is a comment explaining the purpose of this block
of code. It's used for formal documentation or as a reminder.
*/
```

While both single-line and multi-line comments are useful, it's more common to use single-line comments in your everyday coding.

---

start

## Preventing Execution with Comments

Comments can be used to temporarily prevent the execution of specific lines or blocks of code when testing alternative solutions. Simply add `//` at the beginning of a line to turn it into a comment:

```
code;
// code to test
more code;
```

Alternatively, you can use a multi-line comment to disable multiple lines of code:

```
/* code to test */
more code;
```

---

---

## Exploring JavaScript Comments Further

[![Watch on YouTube](https://www.youtube.com/youtube_logo_dark.png)](https://youtu.be/8yroEebhaEk&list=PLP9IO4UYNF0WWmZpE3W33vVPRl2GvjEqz)
  

---

Note: The `start` and `stop` sections are placeholders, as the original content had these labels.

Here is the rephrased content in Markdown format, maintaining structure and key information:

# JavaScript Variables

### Containers for Storing Data

JavaScript allows declaring variables in four ways:

* Automatically
* Using `var`
* Using `let`
* Using `const`

In this example, `x`, `y`, and `z` are undeclared variables that automatically get declared when first used.

**Note**: It's good practice to declare variables before use.

From the examples, you can infer:

* `x` stores the value 5
* `y` stores the value 6
* `z` stores the value 11

stop
start

### The Evolution of Variable Declaration

The `var` keyword was used in JavaScript code from 1995 to 2015.

The `let` and `const` keywords were introduced in 2015.

Use `const` if the value should not be changed, or if the type should not be changed (arrays and objects).

Use `let` only when you can't use `const`.

Use `var` only when supporting older browsers is necessary.

stop
start
stop
start

### Constant Values

The variables `price1` and `price2` are declared with the `const` keyword, making them constant values that cannot be changed.

The variable `total` is declared with the `let` keyword, allowing its value to change.

## When to Use `var`, `let`, or `const`

1. Always declare variables.
2. Use `const` for constant values.
3. Use `const` for types that should not change (arrays and objects).
4. Use `let` only when you can't use `const`.
5. Use `var` only for backward compatibility.

stop

---

### Algebraic Concepts

Just like in algebra, variables hold values and are used in expressions.

From the example, you can infer that the total is calculated to be 11.

**Note**: Variables store values.

stop

---

## Unique Names

All JavaScript **variables** must have unique names called **identifiers**.

Identifiers can be short or descriptive.

The rules for constructing variable names are:

* Names can contain letters, digits, underscores, and dollar signs.
* Names must start with a letter.
* Names cannot begin with reserved words.
* Names are case-sensitive.

## Note: JavaScript identifiers are case-sensitive.

stop

---

### Assignment Operator

In JavaScript, the equal sign (`=`) is an "assignment" operator, not an "equal to" operator.

This means `x = x + 5` assigns the result of `x + 5` to `x`.

**Note**: The "equal to" operator in JavaScript is written as `==`.

stop

---

## Data Types

JavaScript variables can hold numbers and text values (strings).

Strings are enclosed in quotes, while numbers are not.

If you enclose a number in quotes, it becomes a string.

### Declaring Variables

To declare a JavaScript variable, use the `var` or `let` keyword:

You can also assign a value to the variable when declaring it:

**Note**: It's good practice to declare all variables at the beginning of a script.

stop

---

## Multiple Variables

You can declare multiple variables in one statement using commas:

A declaration can span multiple lines:

stop

---

### Undefined Values

In computer programs, variables are often declared without values. The value is calculated later or provided by user input.

Variables declared without values have the value `undefined`.

The variable `carName` will remain `undefined` after this declaration:

stop

---

## Re-Declaring Variables

You can re-declare a `var` variable without losing its value.

However, you cannot re-declare a `let` or `const` variable.

# JavaScript Let Variables

## Introduction to Let Variables

The `let` keyword was introduced in ECMAScript 6 (2015) and is used to declare variables with block scope. This means that the scope of a variable declared with `let` is limited to the block it is defined within.

### Block Scope

Prior to ES6, JavaScript did not have block scope. Instead, variables had either global scope or function scope. With the introduction of `let` and `const`, JavaScript now has two new scopes: block scope and module scope.

## Variables with Let

Variables declared with `let` must be declared before use. Attempting to use a variable declared with `let` before it is declared will result in an error.

### Redeclaring Variables

Once a variable is declared with `let`, it cannot be redeclared within the same scope. This helps prevent accidental variable redeclarations, which can lead to unexpected behavior.

## Redeclaring and Reassigning Variables

Variables declared with `var` can be redeclared and reassigned. However, this can lead to unexpected behavior, especially when used in conjunction with functions.

In contrast, variables declared with `let` cannot be redeclared within the same scope. This helps maintain predictable behavior and prevents accidental variable redeclarations.

## Comparison of Var, Let, and Const

Here is a comparison of the key features of `var`, `let`, and `const`:

| Feature | var | let | const |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Scope | Global or Function | Block | Block |
| Redeclaration | Allowed | Not allowed | Not allowed |
| Reassignment | Allowed | Allowed | Not allowed |

## Browser Support

The `let` and `const` keywords are supported in modern browsers, including Chrome 49, Edge 12, Firefox 36, Safari 11, and Opera 36.

### Redeclaring Variables

Redeclaring a variable with `var` is allowed anywhere in the program. However, redeclaring a variable with `let` within the same block is not allowed.

## Hoisting of Let Variables

Variables declared with `let` are hoisted to the top of the block, but not initialized. This means that attempting to use a `let` variable before it is declared will result in an error.

### Video: JavaScript let Variables

For more information on using `let` variables in JavaScript, check out this video tutorial:

[![Tutorial on YouTube](https://www.youtube.com/js/images/yt_logo_rgb_dark.png)](https://youtu.be/-rpU6z9O88o&list=PLP9IO4UYNF0WWmZpE3W33vVPRl2GvjEqz)

# JavaScript Const

The `const` keyword was introduced in ES6 (2015) and allows you to declare variables that cannot be reassigned or redeclared.

## Unchangeable Values

A variable defined with the `const` keyword cannot be reassigned. This ensures that once a value is assigned to a constant, it remains unchanged throughout its scope.

## Must Be Assigned

JavaScript `const` variables must be assigned a value when they are declared. This ensures that you can't declare a constant without specifying its initial value.

## When to Use JavaScript Const?

Use `const` when you know that the value should not be changed. This is particularly useful for declaring new arrays, objects, functions, or regular expressions.

---

## Constant References

The `const` keyword doesn't define a constant value; it defines a constant reference to a value. This means you can modify the elements of an array or the properties of an object, but you cannot reassign the entire array or object.

## Constant Arrays

You can change the individual elements of a constant array, but you cannot reassign the entire array.

## Constant Objects

You can add or remove properties from a constant object, but you cannot reassign the entire object.

---

## Scope Comparison

|  | var | let/const |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Redeclaration | Yes | No |
| Reassignment | Yes | No |
| Block Scope | No | Yes |

---

## Browser Support

The `let` and `const` keywords are supported in modern browsers, including Chrome 49, Edge 12, Firefox 36, Safari 11, and Opera 36.

---

## Hoisting

Variables defined with `var` are hoisted to the top of their scope. This means you can use a variable before it's declared. However, variables defined with `const` are also hoisted but not initialized. Using an uninitialized `const` variable will result in a `ReferenceError`.

---

## Redeclaring

You can redeclare a variable with `var`, but you cannot redeclared or reassigned a variable with `let` or `const`. However, you can declare a new variable with the same name in a different scope.

# JavaScript Operators

JavaScript operators are used to perform various mathematical and logical operations on values.

### Examples:

The **Assignment Operator** (`=`) is used to assign a value to a variable, such as `x = 5;`

The **Addition Operator** (`+`) adds numbers together, like `3 + 4 = 7`

The **Multiplication Operator** (`*`) multiplies numbers, like `2 * 3 = 6`

The **Comparison Operator** (`>`, `<`, `==`, etc.) compares values and returns a boolean result

---

## JavaScript Operators

There are several types of JavaScript operators:

* Arithmetic Operators
* Assignment Operators
* Comparison Operators
* String Operators
* Logical Operators
* Bitwise Operators
* Ternary Operators
* Type Operators

These operators can be used to perform various operations, such as addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, exponentiation, and more.

---

## JavaScript Arithmetic Operators

Arithmetic operators are used to perform arithmetic operations on numbers:

| Operator | Description |
| --- | --- |
| + | Addition |
| - | Subtraction |
| \* | Multiplication |
| / | Division |
| % | Modulus (Division Remainder) |
| ++ | Increment |
| -- | Decrement |

---

## JavaScript Assignment Operators

Assignment operators are used to assign values to variables:

| Operator | Example | Same As |
| --- | --- | --- |
| = | x = y | x = y |
| += | x += y | x = x + y |
| -= | x -= y | x = x - y |
| \*= | x \*= y | x = x * y |
| /= | x /= y | x = x / y |
| %= | x %= y | x = x % y |

---

## JavaScript Comparison Operators

Comparison operators are used to compare values and return a boolean result:

| Operator | Description |
| --- | --- |
| == | equal to |
| === | equal value and equal type |
| != | not equal |
| !== | not equal value or not equal type |
| > | greater than |
| < | less than |
| >= | greater than or equal to |
| <= | less than or equal to |

---

## JavaScript String Operators

String operators can be used to concatenate strings:

The `+` operator can also be used to add (concatenate) strings, like `"Hello" + "World" = "Hello World"`

The `+=` assignment operator can also be used to add (concatenate) strings, like `x += "hello";`

---

## JavaScript Logical Operators

Logical operators are used to perform logical operations on values:

| Operator | Description |
| --- | --- |
| && | logical and |
| || | logical or |
| ! | logical not |

---

## JavaScript Type Operators

Type operators are used to determine the type of a value:

| Operator | Description |
| --- | --- |
| typeof | Returns the type of a variable |
| instanceof | Returns true if an object is an instance of an object type |

---

## JavaScript Bitwise Operators

Bitwise operators are used to perform bitwise operations on numbers:

| Operator | Description | Example | Same as | Result | Decimal |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| & | AND | 5 & 1 | 0101 & 0001 | 0001 | 1 |
| | | OR | 5 | 1 | 0101 | 0001 | 0101 | 5 |
| ~ | NOT | ~ 5 | ~0101 | 1010 | 10 |
| ^ | XOR | 5 ^ 1 | 0101 ^ 0001 | 0100 | 4 |
| << | left shift | 5 << 1 | 0101 << 1 | 1010 | 10 |
| >> | right shift | 5 >> 1 | 0101 >> 1 | 0010 | 2 |
| >>> | unsigned right shift | 5 >>> 1 | 0101 >>> 1 | 0010 | 2 |

Note that bitwise operators work on 32-bit signed numbers, so the results may vary depending on the type of operation and the values involved.
